summary: Jeff Atwood explores Alistair Cockburn's concept of software development as a cooperative game rather than a science or traditional engineering discipline. The post contrasts different 'games' being played: corporate projects where career advancement trumps delivery, and open-source development which operates as an infinite game without resource constraints. Atwood extends this to argue that fun and engagement are essential elements of software development, connecting Raph Koster's game design theories to application development. The core argument is that understanding software development through the lens of games provides better insights into team dynamics, resource allocation, and user engagement.
key_points: Software development is neither science nor engineering but a cooperative game of speed and competition against time and other teams
Military/corporate software projects often fail because participants are playing career-advancement games rather than delivery games
Open-source development succeeds differently because it's an infinite game without resource limits or fixed endpoints
Fun and engagement principles from game design apply to all software development, not just games
Social media and web applications like Amazon, eBay, and LinkedIn are essentially massively distributed games with ladders to climb
money_quotes: Software development is a 'game,' a game of speed and cooperation within your team, in competition against other teams.

The logic of the funny contractor behavior doesn't make sense until you realize they are playing a different game, in which different moves are called for. Then it suddenly all makes sense - even if you don't like it.
The game is interesting as long as it is interesting. Any number of players may show up, and they are not on a time-line.
We play games because we derive some kind of essential satisfaction from playing them.
Designing for fun isn't just important for game developers. It's important for all software developers.
Do users want to use your application, or are they forced to use it?
As soon as you give people a ladder to climb, they'll climb it.
Software development is a collaborative game that you play, willingly or not, with your team and your users.
key_insight: Understanding that different stakeholders in software projects are often playing entirely different games—career advancement vs. delivery vs. intrinsic satisfaction—explains dysfunctional behavior and suggests that designing for fun and engagement is essential for both teams and users.
